title: Blood on Silk
timeEstimate: Pro-lane within hours; cold water blot only at home
stainChemistry: Blood contains proteins that bond to silk fast and turn brown over hours as the iron in the blood oxidizes — once it goes brown, it grips harder. Silk is itself a protein fiber, so enzyme cleaners that lift blood from cotton would damage the silk too. Hot water at any step sets the blood AND damages the silk simultaneously. Hydrogen peroxide that works on cotton yellows silk dyes. Cold water, speed, and gentle handling are the only safe options at home.
whyThisWorks: Ice-cold water stops the blood from setting and flushes most of fresh blood through the back of the fabric before it locks in. A silk specialist can then use cold NSD plus a controlled mild acid plus a silk-safe reducing bleach to clear what's left without damaging the fiber. Reducing bleach is the chemistry home can't replicate safely — it pulls oxidized brown blood back to a removable state on silk where peroxide would yellow the dye. Home goal is extraction plus delivery; the cleaner does the rest.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): blood is blood pigment + blood proteins + iron. blood pigment oxidizes from Fe²⁺ (bright red) to Fe³⁺ (brown) over hours, which is why fresh blood is much easier than aged. Silk is fibroin protein — enzyme spotters cleave the same peptide bonds. Sodium hydrosulfite reduces Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ at controlled dilution and dwell, releasing oxidized blood pigment from silk safely.
defaultAssumption: Silk + blood = pro within hours. Home is cold-water blot and delivery to a specialist. Cold-water rule is the difference between salvageable and permanent.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold/ice water, white catch towel, gloves
spottingProtocol.instruction: Gloves on (biohazard). Flush cold water through the BACK of the stain over a white catch towel. Press, no scrubbing. Most fresh blood pigment transfers in the first 60–90 seconds when caught immediately.
Cold NSD sponge press only — never enzyme on silk. NSD suspends remaining blood pigment without alkalinity that would weaken silk.
Diluted acid breaks iron-protein bonds. STRICT 60-sec ceiling on silk. Flush cold immediately.
For oxidized brown blood, reducing bleach reverses Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ — silk-safe at controlled dilution. Strict 60-sec dwell.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cold Water Final Rinse + Press-Dry Flat
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush until clear. Press between dry towels. Lay flat to air-dry, never hang wet.

steps: 1. Gloves. Cold water back-flush at first contact.
2. Cold NSD sponge press, no enzyme on silk.
3. Acetic acid spotter, 60-sec ceiling, cold rinse.
4. Sodium hydrosulfite for oxidized blood, 60-sec ceiling.
5. Final cold rinse, press flat to air-dry.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold throughout — silk sets above 30°C, blood sets above 30°C
warnings: NEVER enzymes on silk — Protein digests silk protein like blood
NEVER hot water — sets silk + sets blood permanently
NEVER hydrogen peroxide on silk — yellows dyes

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Is the Whole Home Protocol
homeSolutions.instruction: Blood on silk has the narrowest home window of any stain on the worst fiber. Two facts collide: silk IS protein fiber (so enzyme cleaners that work on blood from cotton DIGEST the silk itself), and hydrogen peroxide YELLOWS silk dyes. The household stain-removal toolkit doesn't apply here. Home protocol = cold-water blot, get to a pro within hours. Do not improvise.

homeSolutions.agent: Gloves + Cold Water Blot from the BACK
homeSolutions.instruction: Disposable gloves on (biohazard, especially if not your blood). Turn the silk inside out, place a white towel under the stain, run COLD water (ice cold preferred) through the BACK of the stain into the catch towel. Cold ONLY — anything warmer than tap-cold sets the blood AND sets silk. Pour through the fabric, not on top — water rings on silk are as visible as blood. 60 seconds of patient flushing usually pulls fresh blood to a pale shadow.

homeSolutions.agent: Press Dry — DO NOT Apply Anything Else
homeSolutions.instruction: Press silk between two dry white towels to lift moisture. No wringing, no twisting, no hanging wet. Lay flat at room temperature out of sun. Do NOT apply enzyme cleaner (digests silk), peroxide (yellows silk), OxiClean (weakens silk), bleach, or detergent. Every common blood-removal tool damages silk worse than the stain.

CALL FIRST — "Do you treat blood on silk with sodium hydrosulfite?" If they don't know what that is, try another cleaner. When you bring it in, tell them: "Blood on silk, age [hours], cold-water back-flush ONLY at home. No enzymes, no peroxide, no detergent applied." Pro uses cold NSD + acetic acid + reducing bleach with timed dwell — chemistry that's silk-safe and home-impossible.

steps: 2. Gloves on. Cold-water back-flush from the reverse, into a catch towel.
3. Press dry between white towels — no other chemistry.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop after the cold-water back-flush. Anything else home-applied risks worse damage than the blood.
warnings: Biohazard — gloves + sealed disposal of contaminated cloths
NEVER enzymes on silk — digests the fiber
NEVER hot water — sets blood + sets silk
NEVER hydrogen peroxide on silk — yellows dyes
Pour through, not on top — water rings

neverDo: Never enzymes on silk — digests silk protein
Never hot water on silk OR blood
Never bare-handed if blood isn't your own
fiberSensitivities: Silk is protein fiber — same enzyme-digestion as blood protein

materialWarnings: Pro-lane within hours — cold-water blot is the entire home protocol
Enzyme cleaners that work on cotton DIGEST silk
Peroxide that works on cotton YELLOWS silk dyes
Cold + press + pro is the only safe path

escalation.when: Always — silk + blood is pro-lane within hours, regardless of stain age. Earlier for oxidized blood (already brown).
escalation.whatToTell: "Blood on silk, age [hours / overnight / older], cold-water back-flush ONLY at home. No enzymes, no peroxide, no detergent applied. [Color: still red / drifting brown]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in silk (call ahead — confirm sodium hydrosulfite + acetic acid spotting)

contextQuestions: How long ago — minutes, hours, or older?
Color — still bright red, or drifting brown (oxidized)?
Type of silk — charmeuse / chiffon / habotai / dupioni?
Has anyone applied anything yet (peroxide, enzyme cleaner, salt, etc.)?
Whose blood (impacts biohazard handling for the cleaner)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Oxidized brown blood is much harder than fresh red — needs reducing bleach, which is pro-only on silk. Thinner silks (chiffon, habotai) more vulnerable. Pre-applied enzyme cleaner is the worst case — may have already begun to digest the silk protein. Hydrogen peroxide pre-applied = silk likely yellowed.
